  • Hide All Code

Module 06: Computational Text Analysis

Computational Social Science — Graduate & Undergraduate

2026-04-04

1 Overview

Text is the most abundant form of social data. Every newspaper article, court decision, congressional speech, tweet, Reddit post, product review, and clinical note is a record of human communication that potentially contains information about attitudes, values, social relationships, and events. Computational text analysis provides tools for extracting that information at scale.

This module covers the complete text analysis workflow from raw text to interpreted findings. We emphasise understanding what each step does and what choices you are making at every stage — because unlike most statistical methods, text analysis involves many non-obvious, consequential decisions that shape your results.

Learning goals:

  • Build a complete text preprocessing pipeline and explain each decision
  • Compute and visualise word frequencies, keyness, and collocations
  • Conduct sentiment analysis using dictionaries and interpret the output
  • Fit an LDA topic model, interpret the full output, and validate the topics
  • Understand what a topic model can and cannot tell you

2 The Fundamental Challenge: Text to Numbers

Text is unstructured. A regression model expects a matrix of numbers; a corpus of documents is a collection of strings of arbitrary length in arbitrary order. The entire field of natural language processing is about how to convert text into numerical representations without losing too much of what matters.

Every conversion is lossy. When you tokenise text into individual words, you lose word order (grammar, syntax). When you remove stopwords, you lose grammatical context. When you stem words, you conflate words that have related but distinct meanings. When you represent documents as word frequencies (the bag-of-words model), you lose the information that words carry in relation to other words.

None of this means text analysis is wrong — it means you must know what information each step preserves and what it discards, and you must be honest about this in your interpretation.

3 The Dataset: US Presidential Inaugural Addresses

The data_corpus_inaugural corpus is built into quanteda. It contains all US presidential inaugural addresses from 1789 to the present — 59 speeches, ranging from Washington’s second inaugural (135 words) to Obama’s first (2,395 words). It is a useful teaching corpus because: (1) everyone knows roughly what these speeches are about, (2) it spans a very long time period, and (3) the texts are short enough to read.

library(quanteda)
library(quanteda.textstats)
library(quanteda.textplots)

# Load the corpus
corp <- data_corpus_inaugural

cat("=== Presidential Inaugural Addresses ===\n")
## === Presidential Inaugural Addresses ===
cat("Documents:", ndoc(corp), "\n")
## Documents: 60
cat("Earliest:", min(docvars(corp,"Year")), "\n")
## Earliest: 1789
cat("Latest:", max(docvars(corp,"Year")), "\n\n")
## Latest: 2025
# Look at the structure of the corpus metadata
cat("Document variables (metadata):\n")
## Document variables (metadata):
head(docvars(corp)) |> print()
##   Year  President FirstName                 Party
## 1 1789 Washington    George                  none
## 2 1793 Washington    George                  none
## 3 1797      Adams      John            Federalist
## 4 1801  Jefferson    Thomas Democratic-Republican
## 5 1805  Jefferson    Thomas Democratic-Republican
## 6 1809    Madison     James Democratic-Republican
cat("\nFirst 300 characters of Obama 2009:\n")
## 
## First 300 characters of Obama 2009:
cat(substr(as.character(corp["2009-Obama"]), 1, 300), "...\n")
## My fellow citizens:
## 
## I stand here today humbled by the task before us, grateful for the trust you have bestowed, mindful of the sacrifices borne by our ancestors. I thank President Bush for his service to our nation, as well as the generosity and cooperation he has shown throughout this transition.
##  ...

Understanding a corpus object: A corpus in quanteda is a collection of texts with associated metadata (docvars). Think of it as a data frame where one column holds the full text and other columns hold variables about each document (year, speaker, party, etc.). You never modify the corpus — you convert it to a document-feature matrix (DFM) for analysis.

4 Part A: Preprocessing — Every Decision Matters

4.1 The Full Pipeline

Preprocessing converts raw text into an analysable representation. Each step has a rationale and trade-offs. Here is the standard pipeline with justification for each step:

# Step 1: Tokenise — split text into individual tokens (words, punctuation, etc.)
toks <- tokens(
  corp,
  what           = "word",          # tokenise by word (alternatives: character, sentence)
  remove_punct   = TRUE,            # remove punctuation (.,:;!? etc.)
  remove_symbols = TRUE,            # remove @, #, emojis etc.
  remove_numbers = TRUE,            # remove standalone numbers (1776, 2024)
  remove_url     = FALSE,           # keep URLs if present (inaugural speeches: none)
  remove_separators = TRUE          # remove whitespace tokens
)

cat("After tokenisation:\n")
## After tokenisation:
cat("  Tokens in corpus:", format(sum(ntoken(toks)), big.mark=","), "\n")
##   Tokens in corpus: 140,464
cat("  Types (unique tokens):", format(ntype(toks), big.mark=","), "\n")
##   Types (unique tokens):   617    91   819   711   799   531   536 1,034 1,244   997   513   494 1,303 1,886 1,327   491 1,158   937 1,064   353   479   543   824 1,009   671 1,341   818 1,222   839   401 1,428   651   544 1,162 1,216 1,085   739   715   517   267   777   883   616   557   559   737   536   516   894   906   787   637   766   613   763   931   804   573   797   990
cat("  First 20 tokens of Lincoln 1861:\n")
##   First 20 tokens of Lincoln 1861:
print(toks[["1861-Lincoln"]][1:20])
##  [1] "Fellow-Citizens" "of"              "the"             "United"         
##  [5] "States"          "In"              "compliance"      "with"           
##  [9] "a"               "custom"          "as"              "old"            
## [13] "as"              "the"             "Government"      "itself"         
## [17] "I"               "appear"          "before"          "you"
# Step 2: Remove stopwords — very frequent words that carry little content meaning
# "the", "and", "of", "a", "in" appear everywhere and distinguish nothing

stopwords_en <- stopwords("en")    # 175 words from the Snowball stopword list
cat("Sample stopwords:", paste(stopwords_en[1:20], collapse=", "), "\n\n")
## Sample stopwords: i, me, my, myself, we, our, ours, ourselves, you, your, yours, yourself, yourselves, he, him, his, himself, she, her, hers
toks_clean <- tokens_remove(toks, pattern=stopwords_en, padding=FALSE)

cat("After stopword removal:\n")
## After stopword removal:
cat("  Tokens remaining:", format(sum(ntoken(toks_clean)), big.mark=","), "\n")
##   Tokens remaining: 67,096
cat("  Types remaining:", format(ntype(toks_clean), big.mark=","), "\n")
##   Types remaining:   516    58   709   608   684   440   444   905 1,111   886   436   401 1,173 1,744 1,191   416 1,033   826   921   266   396   448   724   892   576 1,212   719 1,108   730   316 1,293   552   443 1,042 1,088   964   636   605   427   201   670   779   523   462   452   627   430   418   757   780   659   532   652   512   656   813   690   467   663   867

Stopword removal trade-offs: Stopwords are the most frequent words in a language. Removing them reduces noise and makes content words more visible. But stopwords are not always meaningless — Mosteller & Wallace (1964) famously identified the authorship of disputed Federalist Papers by analysing stopword frequencies. For content-based analyses (topic models, sentiment), remove them. For stylometric analysis (who wrote what), keep them.

# Step 3: Convert to Document-Feature Matrix (DFM)
# DFM: rows = documents, columns = features (words/tokens), cells = counts
dfm_all <- dfm(toks_clean)

cat("\n=== Document-Feature Matrix ===\n")
## 
## === Document-Feature Matrix ===
cat("Dimensions:", dim(dfm_all), "(documents × features)\n")
## Dimensions: 60 9359 (documents × features)
cat("Sparsity:", round(sparsity(dfm_all)*100, 1),
    "% (proportion of cells with value zero)\n\n")
## Sparsity: 92.7 % (proportion of cells with value zero)
cat("Interpretation: A DFM is almost always very sparse because most words\n")
## Interpretation: A DFM is almost always very sparse because most words
cat("appear in only a few documents. The rare words add noise.\n")
## appear in only a few documents. The rare words add noise.
# Step 4: Trim rare features — keep only words appearing in ≥3 documents
dfm_trim <- dfm_trim(dfm_all, min_docfreq=3, min_termfreq=3)
cat("\nAfter trimming (min_docfreq=3, min_termfreq=3):\n")
## 
## After trimming (min_docfreq=3, min_termfreq=3):
cat("Features remaining:", nfeat(dfm_trim), "\n")
## Features remaining: 3687
cat("Dropped:", nfeat(dfm_all) - nfeat(dfm_trim), "rare features\n")
## Dropped: 5672 rare features

Trimming rationale: Words appearing in only one or two documents are unlikely to help identify patterns that generalise. They also inflate the DFM and slow computation. The threshold (here: ≥3 documents) is a choice — more aggressive trimming loses potentially informative rare terms; less trimming retains noise. Always report the threshold you used.

5 Part B: Frequency Analysis

5.1 What Are the Most Common Words?

The simplest analysis: what words appear most frequently, and does their frequency pattern tell us something substantive?

# Top features overall
top_words <- topfeatures(dfm_trim, n=25)

cat("Top 25 words across all inaugural addresses:\n")
## Top 25 words across all inaugural addresses:
tibble(Word=names(top_words), Count=as.integer(top_words)) |>
  mutate(Rank=row_number()) |>
  dplyr::select(Rank, Word, Count) |>
  kbl(caption="Most Frequent Words — All Inaugural Addresses") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","condensed"), full_width=FALSE)
Most Frequent Words — All Inaugural Addresses
Rank Word Count
1 people 592
2 government 575
3 us 507
4 can 489
5 must 377
6 upon 371
7 great 354
8 may 343
9 states 343
10 world 329
11 nation 324
12 country 323
13 shall 316
14 every 309
15 one 274
16 peace 259
17 new 256
18 power 246
19 now 238
20 public 227
21 time 223
22 america 220
23 citizens 217
24 united 211
25 constitution 210 
# Word cloud — the classic visualisation for word frequency
# (Good for exploration; too imprecise for publication)
textplot_wordcloud(
  dfm_trim,
  max_words   = 80,
  color       = c("#BDC3C7","#7F8C8D","#2C6FAC","#1A5276","#0B2F4A"),
  min_size    = 0.8,
  max_size    = 5.5
)
title("Most Frequent Words — Presidential Inaugural Addresses (1789–2021)",
      cex.main=0.85)

Reading a word cloud: Word size encodes frequency. This is useful for a quick overview but poor for precise comparisons — our visual system is bad at judging size differences. For quantitative comparison, use bar charts. Word clouds are fine for exploration and presentation to non-specialist audiences.

5.2 Frequency Plots by Era

# Add era variable to documents
docvars(corp, "Era") <- cut(docvars(corp,"Year"),
  breaks=c(1788,1860,1933,1980,2025),
  labels=c("Founding era\n(1789–1860)","Industrial era\n(1861–1932)",
           "Modern era\n(1933–1980)","Contemporary\n(1981–2021)"))

dfm_era <- dfm_all |>
  dfm_group(groups=docvars(corp,"Era")) |>
  dfm_weight(scheme="prop")  # convert to proportions (relative frequency)

# Most distinctive top words per era using keyness
era_keyness <- map_dfr(levels(docvars(corp,"Era")), function(era) {
  ks <- textstat_keyness(dfm_era, target=era)
  head(ks, 8) |>
    mutate(era=era) |>
    dplyr::select(feature, chi2, p, n_target, era)
}) |>
  mutate(era=factor(era, levels=levels(docvars(corp,"Era"))))

ggplot(era_keyness, aes(x=chi2, y=reorder_within(feature, chi2, era), fill=era)) +
  geom_col(show.legend=FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~era, scales="free_y", ncol=2) +
  scale_y_reordered() +
  scale_fill_manual(values=css_palette(4)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Most Distinctive Words by Presidential Era (Keyness Analysis)",
    subtitle = "χ² = how much more frequent a word is in this era vs. all others combined.",
    x="Keyness (χ²)", y=NULL,
    caption  = "Source: data_corpus_inaugural. quanteda package."
  ) +
  theme(strip.text=element_text(face="bold"))

Reading keyness: Keyness measures how much more (or less) frequent a word is in a target corpus compared to a reference corpus, correcting for overall word frequency. A high χ² for “freedom” in the Contemporary era means presidents have used that word substantially more than would be expected given its overall frequency. Keyness reveals what makes an era linguistically distinctive — not just what is common, but what is unusually common.

6 Part C: Sentiment Analysis

6.1 What Dictionary-Based Sentiment Analysis Measures

Dictionary-based sentiment analysis works by matching words in your text against a pre-defined list of positive and negative words. The Lexicoder Sentiment Dictionary (LSD), designed specifically for political text, classifies words as positive or negative based on their political-domain meaning.

What it actually measures: The relative prevalence of words that the dictionary classifies as positive or negative. This is a proxy for sentiment — it measures the presence of sentiment words, not the full emotional content of the text (which depends on syntax, context, irony, and negation that dictionaries cannot capture).

When it is valid: When the dictionary was validated on text from the same domain, time period, and context as your corpus. The LSD was developed and validated on political news and speeches — making it appropriate for inaugural addresses. Using a general-purpose dictionary (e.g., AFINN, Bing) on political text can produce systematically biased results.

# Apply the Lexicoder Sentiment Dictionary (built into quanteda)
lsd <- data_dictionary_LSD2015

# Apply dictionary to the corpus using dfm_lookup
dfm_sent <- tokens(corp, remove_punct=TRUE) |>
  dfm() |>
  dfm_lookup(dictionary=lsd) |>
  convert(to="data.frame") |>
  as_tibble() |>
  left_join(docvars(corp) |> rownames_to_column("doc_id"),
            by="doc_id")

cat("Dictionary categories in LSD2015:\n")
## Dictionary categories in LSD2015:
cat(paste(names(lsd), collapse=", "), "\n\n")
## negative, positive, neg_positive, neg_negative
# Compute net sentiment = (positive - neg_positive - negative + neg_negative) / total_words
# neg_positive = "not good" → effectively negative
# neg_negative = "not bad" → effectively positive
dfm_sent <- dfm_sent |>
  mutate(
    # Document word count (for normalisation)
    n_words     = ntoken(tokens(corp[doc_id], remove_punct=TRUE)),
    # Net sentiment: positive minus negative, handling negation categories
    net_sent    = (positive + neg_negative - negative - neg_positive) / n_words,
    # Sentiment polarity (gross positivity rate)
    pct_pos     = (positive + neg_negative) / n_words * 100,
    pct_neg     = (negative + neg_positive) / n_words * 100
  )

cat("Sentiment statistics across all speeches:\n")
## Sentiment statistics across all speeches:
dfm_sent |>
  dplyr::select(net_sent, pct_pos, pct_neg) |>
  summary() |>
  print()
##     net_sent            pct_pos          pct_neg     
##  Min.   :-0.008584   Min.   : 5.293   Min.   :1.838  
##  1st Qu.: 0.039423   1st Qu.: 7.682   1st Qu.:3.075  
##  Median : 0.047671   Median : 8.515   Median :3.597  
##  Mean   : 0.048871   Mean   : 8.662   Mean   :3.775  
##  3rd Qu.: 0.061139   3rd Qu.: 9.756   3rd Qu.:4.221  
##  Max.   : 0.098346   Max.   :12.500   Max.   :7.273
ggplot(dfm_sent |> filter(!is.na(Year)),
       aes(x=Year, y=net_sent*100)) +
  geom_line(colour="#2C6FAC", linewidth=0.8, alpha=0.7) +
  geom_point(aes(colour=net_sent), size=3) +
  geom_smooth(method="loess", span=0.5, se=TRUE,
              colour="#C0392B", fill="#F9C0C0", linewidth=1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept=0, linetype="dashed", colour="grey50") +
  scale_colour_gradient2(low="#C0392B", mid="#F7DC6F", high="#27AE60",
                         midpoint=0, guide="none") +
  geom_text(aes(label=ifelse(net_sent < quantile(net_sent,.1,na.rm=T)|
                               net_sent > quantile(net_sent,.9,na.rm=T),
                             President,"")),
            hjust=-0.1, size=2.8, colour="grey30") +
  labs(
    title    = "Net Sentiment in Presidential Inaugural Addresses, 1789–2021",
    subtitle = paste("Net sentiment = (positive − negative words) / total words × 100.",
                     "Red line = LOESS trend with 95% CI.",
                     "\nPoints above 0 = more positive than negative language."),
    x="Year", y="Net sentiment (% positive minus % negative)",
    caption  = "Source: data_corpus_inaugural. Dictionary: LSD2015."
  ) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor=element_blank())

Interpreting sentiment output carefully:

Before concluding anything, ask: Is this measuring what I think it is? Consider Lincoln’s inaugural addresses. They were delivered in extraordinarily fraught circumstances (eve of civil war), yet dictionary-based sentiment might score them moderately positive because Lincoln used formal, measured language. Trump’s 2017 inaugural (“American carnage”) might score as highly negative — but was it? In context, it expressed a populist critique, not despair.

Always read a sample of documents in each extreme of your sentiment distribution to verify that the scores correspond to what you would expect.

7 Part D: Topic Modelling (LDA)

7.1 What LDA Assumes

Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) is a probabilistic generative model for document collections. It assumes:

  1. Each document is a mixture of topics. Lincoln’s 1865 inaugural might be 60% about war, 30% about unity, 10% about law.
  2. Each topic is a distribution over words. The “war” topic assigns high probability to words like “battle,” “victory,” “peace”; lower probability to words like “commerce,” “agriculture.”
  3. Documents were generated by: for each word, (a) draw a topic from the document’s topic mixture, then (b) draw a word from that topic’s word distribution.

LDA works backwards from the observed words to infer the latent topic structure that best explains the corpus.

What LDA does NOT do:

  • It does not know how many topics there are — you must specify K.
  • It does not name the topics — you must interpret them from the top words.
  • It does not know if the topics are coherent or meaningful — that requires human judgment.
  • It does not account for word order, syntax, or semantic relationships between words.

7.2 Building the DFM for LDA

LDA requires a DFM with some additional preprocessing considerations:

# For LDA: stem words to reduce vocabulary (reduce "govern", "government",
# "governance" to the same stem "govern")
toks_lda <- tokens(corp, remove_punct=TRUE, remove_numbers=TRUE) |>
  tokens_remove(stopwords("en")) |>
  tokens_wordstem(language="en")    # Porter stemmer

dfm_lda <- dfm(toks_lda) |>
  dfm_trim(min_docfreq=3, min_termfreq=4)  # slightly stricter for LDA

# LDA requires a document-term matrix with at least 1 token per document
dfm_lda <- dfm_lda[rowSums(dfm_lda) > 0, ]

cat("DFM for LDA:\n")
## DFM for LDA:
cat("  Documents:", nrow(dfm_lda), "\n")
##   Documents: 60
cat("  Features:", ncol(dfm_lda), "\n")
##   Features: 2346
cat("  Total tokens:", sum(dfm_lda), "\n")
##   Total tokens: 62132
# Convert to topicmodels format
dtm_lda <- convert(dfm_lda, to="topicmodels")

7.3 Selecting K — The Number of Topics

# Fit LDA for several values of K, compare perplexity
# Perplexity measures how well the model predicts held-out documents
# Lower perplexity = better fit (but doesn't penalise complexity)

set.seed(2024)
k_values <- c(3, 5, 7, 10)

perplexities <- map_dbl(k_values, function(k) {
  m <- LDA(dtm_lda, k=k, method="Gibbs",
           control=list(seed=2024, burnin=1000, iter=2000, thin=5))
  perplexity(m, newdata=dtm_lda)
})

tibble(K=k_values, Perplexity=round(perplexities,2)) |>
  kbl(caption="LDA Perplexity by Number of Topics") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","condensed"), full_width=FALSE) |>
  footnote(general=paste(
    "Lower perplexity = better statistical fit, but more topics always reduces perplexity.",
    "\nChoose K based on: perplexity plateau, topic coherence, and substantive interpretability.",
    "\nA topic model with perfect perplexity but unintelligible topics is useless."
  ))
LDA Perplexity by Number of Topics
K Perplexity
3 972.82
5 943.89
7 925.49
10 903.41
Note:
Lower perplexity = better statistical fit, but more topics always reduces perplexity.
Choose K based on: perplexity plateau, topic coherence, and substantive interpretability.
A topic model with perfect perplexity but unintelligible topics is useless.

7.4 Fitting the LDA Model

set.seed(2024)
K <- 5   # start with 5 — a reasonable number for a corpus of 59 speeches

lda_model <- LDA(
  dtm_lda,
  k       = K,
  method  = "Gibbs",
  control = list(
    seed    = 2024,
    burnin  = 1000,     # iterations before sampling begins (discard these)
    iter    = 2000,     # sampling iterations
    thin    = 5,        # keep every 5th sample (reduce autocorrelation)
    verbose = 0
  )
)

cat("LDA fitted. K =", K, "topics.\n")
## LDA fitted. K = 5 topics.
cat("Log-likelihood:", round(logLik(lda_model), 2), "\n")
## Log-likelihood: -385994.3

7.5 Reading the LDA Output — Top Words Per Topic

# beta matrix: P(word | topic) — how strongly each word is associated with each topic
# This is the primary output to interpret

topic_words <- tidy(lda_model, matrix="beta") |>
  group_by(topic) |>
  slice_max(beta, n=12) |>  # top 12 words per topic
  ungroup()

cat("=== TOP WORDS PER TOPIC ===\n")
## === TOP WORDS PER TOPIC ===
cat("Each word's beta = P(word | topic) — its probability under that topic.\n\n")
## Each word's beta = P(word | topic) — its probability under that topic.
topic_words |>
  dplyr::select(topic, term, beta) |>
  mutate(beta=round(beta,5)) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from=topic, values_from=beta,
              names_prefix="Topic_") |>
  head(12) |>
  print()
## # A tibble: 12 × 6
##    term     Topic_1  Topic_2 Topic_3  Topic_4 Topic_5
##    <chr>      <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl>
##  1 law      0.0223  NA       NA      NA       NA     
##  2 upon     0.0200   0.00955 NA      NA       NA     
##  3 govern   0.0162   0.0273  NA      NA       NA     
##  4 peopl    0.0147   0.0114   0.0195 NA       NA     
##  5 shall    0.0127   0.00757 NA      NA       NA     
##  6 must     0.0118  NA       NA       0.0107  NA     
##  7 congress 0.0108  NA       NA      NA       NA     
##  8 secur    0.0106  NA       NA      NA       NA     
##  9 now      0.00986 NA       NA       0.00906 NA     
## 10 polici   0.00928 NA       NA      NA       NA     
## 11 make     0.00837 NA       NA      NA       NA     
## 12 great    0.00762 NA       NA      NA        0.0140
ggplot(topic_words, aes(x=beta, y=reorder_within(term, beta, topic),
                         fill=factor(topic))) +
  geom_col(show.legend=FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~paste("Topic", topic), scales="free_y", ncol=3) +
  scale_y_reordered() +
  scale_fill_manual(values=css_palette(K)) +
  labs(
    title    = paste0("LDA Top Words per Topic (K=", K, ") — Inaugural Addresses"),
    subtitle = paste("Word size ∝ P(word|topic).",
                     "Interpret topics by finding the common theme in top words.",
                     "\nTopics with vague, overlapping words suggest K is too high."),
    x="Word-topic probability (β)", y=NULL,
    caption  = "Source: data_corpus_inaugural. LDA via topicmodels package."
  ) +
  theme(strip.text=element_text(face="bold"))

Reading this plot: Each panel is one topic. Words with longer bars are more characteristic of that topic. Your job is to read the top words and infer the theme. Common patterns:

  • A clear theme: top words cluster around an obvious concept (“nation,” “people,” “government,” “free,” “citizen” → civic republicanism)
  • A time-period topic: words associated with a historical period’s vocabulary rather than a substantive theme
  • A garbage topic: miscellaneous words without clear theme → suggests K is too high or preprocessing was inadequate

Name each topic. Work through the top 15–20 words (not just 10), read 3–5 documents that score highly on that topic, and then assign a label. Document your reasoning.

7.6 Document-Topic Proportions

# gamma matrix: P(topic | document) — how much each document is about each topic
doc_topics <- tidy(lda_model, matrix="gamma") |>
  left_join(
    tibble(document=rownames(dfm_lda),
           Year=docvars(dfm_lda,"Year"),
           President=docvars(dfm_lda,"President")),
    by="document"
  ) |>
  mutate(topic=paste("Topic", topic))

cat("=== DOCUMENT-TOPIC PROPORTIONS (gamma) ===\n")
## === DOCUMENT-TOPIC PROPORTIONS (gamma) ===
cat("Each document has a proportion for each topic; they sum to 1.\n\n")
## Each document has a proportion for each topic; they sum to 1.
doc_topics |>
  filter(President %in% c("Lincoln","Roosevelt","Obama","Trump")) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from=topic, values_from=gamma) |>
  dplyr::select(President, Year, starts_with("Topic")) |>
  mutate(across(starts_with("Topic"), ~round(.x,3))) |>
  kbl(caption="Topic Proportions for Selected Presidents") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover")) |>
  footnote(general="Each row sums to 1. Dominant topic = highest proportion.")
Topic Proportions for Selected Presidents
President Year Topic 1 Topic 2 Topic 3 Topic 4 Topic 5
Lincoln 1861 0.267 0.511 0.066 0.060 0.096
Lincoln 1865 0.084 0.188 0.225 0.247 0.256
Roosevelt 1905 0.137 0.137 0.370 0.212 0.145
Roosevelt 1933 0.255 0.108 0.295 0.216 0.126
Roosevelt 1937 0.170 0.100 0.360 0.276 0.094
Roosevelt 1941 0.067 0.163 0.319 0.351 0.100
Roosevelt 1945 0.100 0.097 0.381 0.291 0.131
Obama 2009 0.066 0.052 0.245 0.522 0.115
Obama 2013 0.063 0.067 0.233 0.539 0.098
Trump 2017 0.082 0.057 0.143 0.641 0.076
Trump 2025 0.136 0.084 0.124 0.547 0.109
Note:
Each row sums to 1. Dominant topic = highest proportion. 
# How has the prevalence of each topic changed over time?
doc_topics |>
  filter(!is.na(Year)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x=Year, y=gamma, colour=topic, group=topic)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=0.9, alpha=0.8) +
  geom_smooth(method="loess", span=0.6, se=FALSE,
              linewidth=0.6, linetype="dashed") +
  scale_colour_manual(values=css_palette(K), name=NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent) +
  labs(
    title    = "Topic Prevalence in Inaugural Addresses Over Time",
    subtitle = paste("Each line = one topic's proportion per document.",
                     "Solid = observed; dashed = LOESS trend.",
                     "\nRising topic = increasingly common theme in inaugurals."),
    x="Year", y="Topic proportion (γ)",
    caption  = "Source: data_corpus_inaugural, LDA K=5."
  ) +
  theme(legend.position="bottom")

8 Validation: Are Your Topics Real?

Topic models always produce topics. The question is whether those topics correspond to real, meaningful thematic structure in the corpus or are statistical artefacts of the model and preprocessing choices.

Validation strategies:

# 1. Read the documents that score highest on each topic
# If a topic is real, the high-scoring documents should have an obvious common theme
cat("=== VALIDATION: Top documents per topic ===\n\n")
## === VALIDATION: Top documents per topic ===
doc_topics |>
  group_by(topic) |>
  slice_max(gamma, n=3) |>
  dplyr::select(topic, President, Year, gamma) |>
  mutate(gamma=round(gamma,3)) |>
  arrange(topic, desc(gamma)) |>
  kbl(caption="Top 3 Documents per Topic (Highest γ)") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover")) |>
  footnote(general=paste(
    "Read the actual speeches for these Presidents.",
    "If the topic's top words describe the speeches accurately,",
    "the topic is externally valid."
  ))
Top 3 Documents per Topic (Highest γ)
topic President Year gamma
Topic 1 Taft 1909 0.545
Topic 1 McKinley 1897 0.452
Topic 1 Harrison 1889 0.413
Topic 2 Harrison 1841 0.584
Topic 2 Lincoln 1861 0.511
Topic 2 Polk 1845 0.459
Topic 3 Truman 1949 0.456
Topic 3 Eisenhower 1957 0.445
Topic 3 Eisenhower 1953 0.437
Topic 4 Clinton 1997 0.673
Topic 4 Biden 2021 0.664
Topic 4 Clinton 1993 0.662
Topic 5 Monroe 1817 0.490
Topic 5 Monroe 1821 0.476
Topic 5 Jefferson 1805 0.434
Note:
Read the actual speeches for these Presidents. If the topic’s top words describe the speeches accurately, the topic is externally valid. 
# 2. Semantic coherence: do the top words of each topic co-occur in the same documents?
# High coherence = words that appear together often = more coherent topic
# Can be computed manually by checking pairwise co-occurrence of top words

# 3. Exclusivity: are the top words exclusive to this topic or shared across topics?
# Exclusive words are more informative about what distinguishes the topic

# Quick check: do any top words appear in multiple topics' top 10?
top_10_per_topic <- tidy(lda_model, matrix="beta") |>
  group_by(topic) |>
  slice_max(beta, n=10) |>
  ungroup()

shared_words <- top_10_per_topic |>
  count(term, name="n_topics") |>
  filter(n_topics > 1) |>
  arrange(desc(n_topics))

if (nrow(shared_words) > 0) {
  cat("Words appearing in multiple topics' top-10 (low exclusivity):\n")
  print(shared_words)
  cat("\nThese words are not distinctive — they appear across topics.\n")
  cat("They may indicate topics are not well separated.\n")
} else {
  cat("No top-10 words appear in multiple topics — good exclusivity.\n")
}
## Words appearing in multiple topics' top-10 (low exclusivity):
## # A tibble: 7 × 2
##   term   n_topics
##   <chr>     <int>
## 1 nation        3
## 2 peopl         3
## 3 govern        2
## 4 must          2
## 5 one           2
## 6 upon          2
## 7 world         2
## 
## These words are not distinctive — they appear across topics.
## They may indicate topics are not well separated.

9 Part E: A Complete Text Analysis Workflow

Here is the entire workflow condensed into annotated steps, showing the decision points:

cat("COMPLETE TEXT ANALYSIS WORKFLOW\n")
## COMPLETE TEXT ANALYSIS WORKFLOW
cat("================================\n\n")
## ================================
cat("1. LOAD CORPUS\n")
## 1. LOAD CORPUS
cat("   data_corpus_inaugural → 59 documents, metadata attached\n\n")
##    data_corpus_inaugural → 59 documents, metadata attached
cat("2. TOKENISE\n")
## 2. TOKENISE
cat("   tokens(..., remove_punct=TRUE) → 59,000 tokens\n")
##    tokens(..., remove_punct=TRUE) → 59,000 tokens
cat("   Decision: what to remove? punctuation (yes), numbers (yes), URLs (no here)\n\n")
##    Decision: what to remove? punctuation (yes), numbers (yes), URLs (no here)
cat("3. CLEAN\n")
## 3. CLEAN
cat("   tokens_remove(stopwords('en')) → removes 'the', 'and', etc.\n")
##    tokens_remove(stopwords('en')) → removes 'the', 'and', etc.
cat("   Decision: which stopword list? ('en' = Snowball, 175 words)\n\n")
##    Decision: which stopword list? ('en' = Snowball, 175 words)
cat("4. REPRESENT\n")
## 4. REPRESENT
cat("   dfm() → document-feature matrix\n")
##    dfm() → document-feature matrix
cat("   dfm_trim(min_docfreq=3) → removes very rare words\n")
##    dfm_trim(min_docfreq=3) → removes very rare words
cat("   Decision: trimming threshold? (depends on corpus size)\n\n")
##    Decision: trimming threshold? (depends on corpus size)
cat("5. ANALYSE (choose based on research question)\n")
## 5. ANALYSE (choose based on research question)
cat("   Frequency/keyness → what words distinguish groups?\n")
##    Frequency/keyness → what words distinguish groups?
cat("   Sentiment → positive/negative balance over time?\n")
##    Sentiment → positive/negative balance over time?
cat("   LDA → what topics structure the corpus?\n\n")
##    LDA → what topics structure the corpus?
cat("6. VALIDATE\n")
## 6. VALIDATE
cat("   Read documents in each extreme. Do they match your interpretation?\n")
##    Read documents in each extreme. Do they match your interpretation?
cat("   Compare topics to external criteria (e.g., presidential party, era)\n\n")
##    Compare topics to external criteria (e.g., presidential party, era)
cat("7. REPORT\n")
## 7. REPORT
cat("   Report preprocessing decisions (all of them)\n")
##    Report preprocessing decisions (all of them)
cat("   Report validation evidence\n")
##    Report validation evidence
cat("   Be explicit about what the analysis can and cannot show\n")
##    Be explicit about what the analysis can and cannot show

10 Common Mistakes

Mistake Consequence Prevention
Treating LDA topics as objective truth Confirmation bias; topics are hypotheses, not findings Always read documents; test against external criteria
Not reporting preprocessing choices Unreplicable results; different choices → different topics Report every preprocessing decision in your methods
Using wrong dictionary for sentiment Systematically biased sentiment estimates Validate dictionary against manual codes from your specific corpus
Choosing K without validation Over- or under-extracted topics Use perplexity + coherence + qualitative inspection together
Not stemming before LDA Vocabulary explosion; “nation” and “nations” treated as different Stem, or use word embeddings that capture morphological similarity

11 Check Your Understanding

1. Remove stopwords from a paragraph of your choosing using tokens_remove(stopwords("en")). Now examine what is left. Are there words that were removed that you would have kept? Are there words that survived that you would have removed? What does this tell you about the limitations of standard stopword lists?

2. Run the full LDA workflow with K=3 and K=7. For each, report the top 10 words per topic and your proposed label. Which K produces more interpretable topics? Explain your reasoning.

3. A topic model of Reddit posts about mental health produces one topic with top words: “feel,” “like,” “just,” “know,” “really,” “think,” “way,” “people,” “make,” “time.” Is this a substantive topic? What does it tell you, and what should you do about it?

4. The sentiment analysis shows Trump’s 2017 inaugural has the most negative net sentiment in the dataset. A student concludes “Trump’s speech expressed pessimism.” Why might this conclusion be wrong? What additional evidence would you need?

5. 🟥 Read Nelson (2020) on Computational Grounded Theory. Apply the three-step framework to the following scenario: you have 10,000 Reddit posts about housing affordability. Describe in concrete terms what you would do in each of the three steps, what decisions you would make, and what you would look for.
